title: EEG Attention Detection System
desc: ML pipeline classifying attention states from EEG brainwave data. Detects when someone is engaged or mentally checked out while viewing visual content. ~64% accuracy across unseen subjects.

detail.summary: Analyzes EEG brainwave data to detect when someone is engaged or mentally checked out while looking at visual content. We were interested in what happens during doomscrolling — where someone keeps watching but is not fully engaged.
detail.why: The goal was to see if brain signals alone can tell the difference between active thinking and passive attention, using only frequency-band features without complex deep learning.
detail.problem: EEG signals are noisy and highly person-specific. Feature choice matters more than model complexity, and real data requires careful preprocessing before any classification is meaningful.
detail.approach: Pipeline: raw EEG recordings -> short time windows -> frequency band features (Theta 4-7 Hz, Alpha 8-12 Hz, Beta 13-30 Hz). Features like alpha power, beta power, and alpha/beta ratio represent different brain states. Higher alpha = more idle. Higher beta = more active thinking. We compared Logistic Regression vs XGBoost. XGBoost reached ~64% accuracy on unseen subjects.
detail.results: ~64% classification accuracy on unseen subjects. Alpha and beta bands were the most useful features. The model generalized across different subjects, showing it learned meaningful patterns rather than overfitting.

sections.title: What we learned
sections.body: Feature choice matters more than model complexity. Different brain states can be separated using frequency bands alone. Real data requires careful preprocessing — without it, accuracy dropped to ~51%, barely above chance.

sections.title: Broader applications
sections.body: These ideas apply to brain-computer interfaces, attention monitoring systems, and adaptive user interfaces where the system adjusts based on the user's cognitive state.
